items.q: What is the difference between CAD and CAM?
items.correct: CAD creates digital designs; CAM uses those designs to control manufacturing machines.
items.misconception: They are the same thing with different names.
items.random_wrong: CAD is for cars, CAM is for cameras.
items.why: Students treat the terms as synonymous because they often appear together. CAD = drawing/modelling software; CAM = machine control (CNC routers, 3D printers) - typically the second stage.

items.q: Why is injection moulding suited to high-volume production?
items.correct: Tooling cost is high but per-unit cost is very low at scale.
items.misconception: It is the cheapest method for any quantity.
items.random_wrong: It is the only way to make plastic items.
items.why: Students think a 'good' process is good for everything. Injection moulding has expensive moulds - it's only economical when costs are spread over thousands or millions of parts.

items.correct: A small integrated chip containing CPU, memory, and I/O for controlling a device.
items.misconception: A very small computer like a laptop.
items.random_wrong: A remote control for a TV.
items.why: Students think microcontrollers are mini-PCs. They are specialised: limited resources, designed for a single embedded task. No operating system needed - just dedicated code running directly.
Correct answer: Standard CNC tool-path language. “A CAD format” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: 5-axis machining adds which two extra axes?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: A, B (rotational). “X, Y” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A 3-axis CNC has axes X, Y, Z. A 5-axis CNC has how many extra axes (beyond 3)?
items.why: The real figure is 2. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
